name: Lyon Mountain
short_description: Historic iron ore mountain in the Adirondacks offering magnetite and garnet specimens. Known for its rich mining heritage and accessible collecting on public land near Dannemora.
description:
Lyon Mountain is a prominent peak in Clinton County, New York, within the transition zone between the Adirondack highlands and surrounding plateaus. This location is renowned for its magnetite deposits, a direct result of the Precambrian iron oxide mineralizations that characterize this region of upstate New York. The mountain was actively mined for iron ore in the 19th and early 20th centuries, leaving behind mineral-rich outcrops and tailings that continue to yield collectible specimens today.

The geology of Lyon Mountain reflects deep metamorphic basement rock, part of the ancient Grenville orogeny that formed the Adirondack Mountains. Magnetite, the primary iron oxide mineral found here, often appears as black metallic crystals and masses within the metamorphic host rock. Associated minerals include garnet and mica, which frequently occur in the schists and gneisses that dominate the region. These minerals formed under high-temperature, high-pressure conditions during the collision of ancient continental plates over a billion years ago.

Visitors to Lyon Mountain can expect a moderately accessible site with relatively flat terrain at the summit elevation. The area provides good opportunities for both new and experienced rockhounders to find magnetite specimens, from small individual crystals to larger masses suitable for display or study. The historical mining context adds educational value to any visit, as visitors can observe the results of past extraction activities and understand the connection between geology and local industrial history.

The collecting here is best done in cooler months when trails are dry and accessible. The combination of historical significance and active mineral availability makes Lyon Mountain a worthwhile destination for those interested in iron oxide minerals and Adirondack geology.
history: Lyon Mountain's magnetite deposits formed during the Grenville orogeny approximately 1.1 billion years ago, when intense tectonic activity and metamorphism concentrated iron oxides within layered metamorphic sequences. The mountain was extensively mined for iron ore during the 1800s and early 1900s, with commercial operations extracting high-grade magnetite ore that was transported to regional iron furnaces and mills.
tips: Search the slopes and old mining areas where exposed rock faces and tailings piles offer the best access to fresh magnetite specimens. Use a magnet to quickly identify magnetite-rich material before picking it up. Look for black metallic crystalline masses within the lighter-colored host schist and gneiss; garnets often appear as reddish translucent crystals in association with magnetite-bearing zones. A small hammer or chisel helps extract specimens from larger outcrops, but collect responsibly and avoid deepening old mine cuts.
terrain_notes: Lyon Mountain reaches 2445 feet elevation with relatively flat terrain at the summit plateau. Slopes vary from gentle to moderate depending on approach route. Vegetation includes mixed forest typical of the northern Adirondacks. The old mining areas feature exposed bedrock, scattered tailings, and some overgrown workings. Footing can be uneven on mineralized outcrops and loose fragments. Parking areas and trail access are generally accessible to hikers in reasonable condition.
best_season: May - October. Spring through fall offers the best conditions for hiking and collecting. Winter snow and ice make the mountain difficult to access safely. Late spring and early fall provide ideal temperatures for extended outdoor work.
written_directions: From the Town of Dannemora, head north on NY Route 374 for approximately 2 miles. Look for the Lyon Mountain access road on the right (east) side. Follow the local forest or access road upslope toward the summit area. Parking is typically available near the base or lower slopes. Distance from Dannemora town center to the mountain base is roughly 3 miles. Note: Verify current access and parking conditions locally, as mountain roads may have seasonal restrictions.
road_conditions: Access roads may be unpaved or rough depending on current maintenance. Passenger vehicle access to lower parking areas, but hiking required to reach upper slopes and mining areas.
parking_notes: Limited parking available near the base of Lyon Mountain. May include a small pullout or informal lot. Capacity unknown; arrive early during peak season.
collection_rules: Personal collection for rockhounding and educational purposes is permitted on publicly accessible portions of Lyon Mountain. Respect private property boundaries. Do not remove large quantities or conduct commercial mining operations. Stay away from active mining areas if any remain operational. Avoid disturbing unstable mine workings or entering abandoned shafts.
rules:
Personal use collection only
No commercial mining or sale
Respect private property boundaries
Do not enter unsafe mine workings
Collect responsibly without excessive site damage
Remove specimens only from loose material or legal collecting areas
nearest_services: Town of Dannemora, approximately 3 miles south. Dannemora offers gas stations, small stores, and local services. Larger shopping and dining in Peru or Plattsburgh, both 8-12 miles away.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with some steep sections. Uneven rocky footing on mining areas and exposed bedrock. Not wheelchair accessible. Sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended. The flat summit area is easier to navigate than the approach slopes. Some visitors may find the steep uphill hike challenging, but the relatively low elevation (2445 feet) is manageable for most people in moderate condition.

faq.question: What minerals can I find rockhounding at Lyon Mountain near Dannemora?
faq.answer: Lyon Mountain is known for magnetite, a black iron oxide mineral that is strongly magnetic. You will also find garnet (reddish crystals) and mica scattered throughout the metamorphic rock. Magnetite is the primary target here, often appearing as metallic masses or crystals in the host schist and gneiss.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Lyon Mountain free or do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Lyon Mountain is free and does not require a permit for personal collection. The site is open to public collecting on accessible land. However, respect private property boundaries and avoid any areas marked as off-limits or unsafe.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Lyon Mountain in New York?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding season at Lyon Mountain is May through October. These months offer dry trails, stable temperatures, and safe hiking conditions. Winter snow and ice make access difficult and potentially dangerous. Late spring and early fall provide ideal weather for extended collecting trips.

faq.question: Is Lyon Mountain rockhounding site suitable for beginners and kids?
faq.answer: Yes, Lyon Mountain is beginner and family-friendly. Kids ages 8 and up can participate with adult supervision. The relatively flat summit plateau and exposed magnetite specimens make it easy to find collectibles. Magnetite is easy to identify with a magnet, perfect for teaching young rockhounders about minerals and magnetic properties.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Lyon Mountain?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand magnet to quickly identify magnetite in the field. A small hammer or chisel helps extract specimens from outcrops. Sturdy hiking boots are essential for the uneven rocky terrain. Bring plenty of water, sunscreen, and a backpack to carry specimens. A field guide to Adirondack minerals is helpful for identifying associated garnets and micas.

faq.question: How far is Lyon Mountain from nearby towns and what are parking conditions?
faq.answer: Lyon Mountain is approximately 3 miles north of Dannemora in Clinton County. Limited parking is available near the mountain base, typically in a small pullout or informal lot. The Town of Dannemora offers basic services and gas stations nearby. Plan to arrive early during peak season as parking is limited. Larger amenities are available in Peru and Plattsburgh, 8-12 miles away.

meta_description: Find magnetite, garnet, and mica at Lyon Mountain near Dannemora, NY. Free public rockhounding on historic iron mountain in the Adirondacks.
